marketOverview: Global markets enter the week under the shadow of Operation Epic Fury — the joint US-Israeli strike on Iranian nuclear and missile infrastructure launched Saturday, March 1. Tehran retaliated with missile strikes on US assets in Bahrain, Middle Eastern airspace is closed, and the Strait of Hormuz faces effective disruption. Brent crude is projected to gap toward $100 at Monday's open, triggering a sharp rotation into USD, JPY, and CHF as premier havens. High-beta currencies (AUD, NZD) face significant gap-down risk, while CAD is caught between energy windfall and domestic weakness. The week's key data — Eurozone CPI Flash, UK Spring Statement, US NFP, and CAD Employment — will determine whether risk sentiment stabilizes or deteriorates further.

currencies.name: US Dollar
currencies.rationale: USD enters the week with a dual haven and rate premium advantage. The weekend escalation in the Middle East reinforces the Dollar's liquidity premium as investors rotate into US Treasuries and the Greenback. Domestically, Core PPI overshot at +0.8% MoM (vs +0.3% exp), Consumer Confidence rose to 91.2 (vs 87.0 exp), and Initial Jobless Claims held firm at 212k. The global tariff increase to 15% under Section 122 adds structural USD support via higher federal revenue expectations and sticky inflation. ISM Manufacturing PMI on Monday and NFP on Friday are the key data tests.
drivers: Operation Epic Fury: US-Iran war risk, Strait of Hormuz disruption
Core PPI +0.8% MoM (vs +0.3% exp, hot)
Consumer Confidence 91.2 (vs 87.0 exp, beat)
Section 122 global tariff raised to 15%
Fed Musalem: ~50% of inflation is tariff-driven
ISM Manufacturing PMI (MON) + NFP (FRI) key tests

currencies.name: Euro
currencies.rationale: EUR faces a difficult week as a large net energy importer exposed to the Strait of Hormuz disruption. Sticky Eurozone inflation (Core HICP 2.2%, French CPI 1.0% vs 0.8% exp) keeps the ECB cautious, but the energy shock threatens to compress real incomes and raise downside growth risks. The EU Parliament is targeting a March approval vote for the EU-US trade deal to avoid escalation, but trade uncertainty remains an overhang. EUR can outperform high-beta currencies in risk-off, but typically lags USD and CHF when liquidity preference dominates. Tuesday's CPI Flash Estimate is the key data release.
drivers: Energy importer: Strait of Hormuz disruption raises oil costs
Eurozone Core HICP 2.2% (sticky, limits ECB easing)
German Ifo 88.6 (beat, rare positive signal)
EU-US trade deal: Parliament targets March approval vote
MFS banking stress: European lender exposure (Santander, Barclays)
ECB Lagarde speech (MON + THU) key for guidance

currencies.name: British Pound
currencies.rationale: GBP enters the week at the epicenter of a domestic and external storm. The collapse of UK lender MFS (fraud, double-pledging) has triggered a localized credit scare, while Labour's historic by-election defeat puts PM Starmer's leadership under scrutiny ahead of the Spring Statement on Tuesday. As a net energy importer, the UK faces a stagflationary shock from the oil spike, complicating the BoE's expectation of being near target by April. British Airways has already suspended Middle Eastern flights. The Spring Statement on Tuesday is the critical domestic catalyst, while BoE guidance (Bailey: April cut 'genuinely open') keeps front end vulnerable.
drivers: MFS lender collapse: fraud, credit contagion risk (Barclays exposed)
Labour by-election defeat: Starmer leadership risk
Energy importer: oil spike = inflationary tax on UK consumer
CBI Distributive Trades -43 (vs -16 exp, sharp contraction)
GfK Consumer Confidence at weakest since Nov 2025
BoE: April cut 'genuinely open' (Bailey)

currencies.name: Japanese Yen
currencies.rationale: JPY is the primary FX beneficiary of the weekend's military escalation. Haven demand and carry trade liquidation are set to dominate the opening sessions, overpowering the domestic political headwinds from PM Takaichi's appointment of reflationist BoJ board members. While Tokyo Core CPI slipped below the BoJ's 2% target (1.8% vs 2.0%), the immediate market reaction rewards safety first. Governor Ueda maintains a hiking bias in parliament, and hawkish dissenter Takata continues to call for gradual further hikes. Japan's energy import sensitivity is a real macro headwind, but it operates with a lag — the initial market reaction is a violent flight into JPY.
drivers: Operation Epic Fury: ultimate haven demand, carry liquidation
PM Takaichi appoints reflationist BoJ board members (dovish headwind)
Tokyo Core CPI 1.8% (below BoJ 2% target for first time since Oct 2024)
BoJ Ueda maintains hiking bias in parliament
BoJ Takata: gradual further hikes needed
USD/JPY peaked at 156.82 on political pressure, then reversed

currencies.name: Australian Dollar
currencies.rationale: AUD enters the week as a high-beta casualty of the Middle East escalation. Despite a briefly supportive trimmed mean CPI (3.4% YoY, above 3.3% exp), the currency's role as a liquid proxy for Chinese growth has become a liability as US-China summit preparations broke down and the PBoC cut the FX risk-reserve ratio to 0% to slow CNH appreciation. The 15% Section 122 tariff removed Australia's prior IEEPA rate advantage, and USTR Greer's new Section 301 investigations into industrial capacity add secondary pressure on Chinese steel and iron ore demand. RBA Bullock speaks Monday — watch for guidance on the March rate path.
drivers: High-beta risk proxy: gap-down risk on Middle East escalation
US-China summit preparations faltered (major AUD headwind)
PBoC cut FX Risk Reserve Ratio to 0% (from 20%)
Section 122 tariff: removed Australia's prior 10% IEEPA advantage
Trimmed Mean CPI 3.4% YoY (above 3.3% exp, briefly supportive)
RBA Bullock (MON) + GDP (WED) key domestic catalysts

currencies.name: Canadian Dollar
currencies.rationale: CAD sits at the crossroads of an energy windfall and domestic weakness. As a primary energy exporter, the Loonie benefits from the oil spike toward $100/bbl — a direct terms-of-trade positive that allows CAD to outperform AUD and NZD in this risk-off phase. However, Q4 GDP contracted at an annualized -0.6% (weakest outside pandemic years), and the BoC's cautious stance leaves front end support less robust than USD. Minister LeBlanc described USMCA conversations as 'constructive', and China confirmed it will not impose tariffs on certain Canadian imports through end-2026. BOC Macklem speaks Wednesday — key for guidance on whether the energy windfall changes the policy calculus.
drivers: Energy windfall: oil toward $100/bbl = direct terms-of-trade positive
Q4 GDP -0.6% annualized (weakest outside pandemic years)
USMCA negotiations: LeBlanc calls talks 'constructive'
China: no tariffs on certain Canadian imports through end-2026
Record foreign buying of Canadian federal bonds in Q4
BOC Macklem Speech (WED) + Ivey PMI (FRI) key events

currencies.name: Swiss Franc
currencies.rationale: CHF reinforces its role as the market's premier safety valve. The Franc outperformed even JPY as the preferred haven last week, as Japan's domestic political appointments diluted the Yen's haven bid. With Operation Epic Fury triggering a full capital preservation rotation, CHF is set for a violent gap higher on Monday. Morgan Stanley projects EUR/CHF down to 0.87, and SNB sight deposits rose to CHF 457.6bln (intervention monitoring). Switzerland's low inflation and the SNB's historical discomfort with excessive strength remain the key qualifier — intervention risk is elevated if CHF appreciation becomes disorderly. Wednesday's CPI print is the key domestic data release.
drivers: Operation Epic Fury: premier capital preservation destination
Outperformed JPY as preferred haven (dovish BoJ appointments diluted JPY bid)
KOF Economic Barometer 104.2 (vs 103 exp, beat)
Q4 GDP +0.2% QoQ (recovery from -0.5% in Q3)
Morgan Stanley: EUR/CHF target 0.87
SNB sight deposits: CHF 457.6bln (intervention monitoring)

currencies.name: New Zealand Dollar
currencies.rationale: NZD enters the week as the most vulnerable G10 currency. The Kiwi underperformed even AUD last week as carry unwinds and trade-related growth fears intensified, and the weekend escalation raises the odds of another defensive leg early in the week. The RBNZ's dovish pivot under Governor Breman creates a domestic rates handicap that is now reinforced by a strong global risk-off impulse. NZD is typically the first place investors cut exposure when volatility rises and carry is reduced, and with no key domestic data this week, the currency is entirely at the mercy of global risk sentiment and China-linked headlines.
drivers: High-beta risk proxy: most vulnerable G10 currency in risk-off
RBNZ dovish pivot: OCR to remain at current levels near term
US-China summit preparations faltered (China = NZD's largest trading partner)
PBoC cut FX Risk Reserve Ratio to 0% (regional FX pressure)
NZD/JPY carry liquidation: Nvidia selloff triggered sharp unwind
No key domestic data this week — entirely sentiment-driven
